metadata.title: Differential Processing of Consonance and Dissonance within the Human Superior Temporal Gyrus
metadata.note: ECoG study with 8 neurosurgical patients (epilepsy), recording directly from lateral surface of temporal lobe. High temporal and spatial resolution for consonance/dissonance processing.

claims.claim_text: Dissonant chords elicit increased high gamma (γhigh, 70-150 Hz) activity compared to consonant chords in superior temporal gyrus (STG) between 75-200 ms post-stimulus onset

axis_proposal.definition: High gamma (70-150 Hz) power in superior temporal gyrus during chord perception, expressed as percent change from baseline
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: ECoG-derived measure of synchronized neuronal firing in STG, linked to local field potentials and neuronal spiking activity

statistics.note: 14 out of 16 electrodes remained significant after FDR correction (q = 0.05). Averaged ERSPs showed significant increase in γhigh activity between 75-200 ms post-stimulus onset (p < 0.001).

brain_regions.context: 29 out of 32 electrodes (91%) with significant activity located within STG. Activity observed as early as 50 ms post-stimulus onset, varying in duration (175-350 ms offset)

methodology.task: Passive listening to consonant and dissonant piano chords (target detection task with cat's meow as target)
methodology.sample_size: 8 subjects (3 left hemisphere, 5 right hemisphere coverage)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Neurosurgical patients with medically intractable epilepsy, right-handed, age 22-68 years

claims.claim_text: Right STG exhibits spatial organization for consonance/dissonance processing, with dissonant-sensitive cortical sites located anterior to non-sensitive sites

axis_proposal.definition: Binary classification of cortical sites as dissonant-sensitive (increased γhigh to dissonant chords) or non-sensitive (no difference between chord types)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Spatial organization of pitch relationship processing in auditory cortex

statistics.note: Spatial distinction significant in right hemisphere but not in left hemisphere (y-dimension: p = 0.67; z-dimension: p = 0.52)

brain_regions.context: Cortical sites exhibiting greater γhigh activity in response to dissonant chords (red electrodes) located anterior to sites with no difference (blue electrodes)
Non-sensitive sites located posterior to dissonant-sensitive sites

methodology.task: Passive listening to consonant and dissonant chords
methodology.participant_characteristics: Neurosurgical patients with right temporal lobe electrode coverage

claims.claim_text: Dissonant type II chords (containing minor second interval) elicit greater high gamma activity than dissonant type I chords (containing major seventh interval) in a subset of dissonant-sensitive cortical sites

axis_proposal.definition: High gamma (70-150 Hz) power in superior temporal gyrus during chord perception
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: ECoG-derived measure of synchronized neuronal firing

statistics.note: 60-66% of dissonant-sensitive sites showed significantly increased γhigh activity in response to dissonant II chords (LH: 4/6 electrodes, 66%; RH: 6/10 electrodes, 60%). 7 out of 10 electrodes remained significant after FDR correction.

brain_regions.context: Subset of dissonant-sensitive cortical sites (10 electrodes total) showed increased activity to dissonant II chords between 75-200 ms post-stimulus onset

methodology.task: Passive listening to two types of dissonant chords (dissonant I: major seventh interval; dissonant II: minor second interval)

claims.claim_text: High gamma power in STG is positively correlated with degree of stimulus roughness, with correlation coefficients of r = 0.43 (right hemisphere) and r = 0.41 (left hemisphere)

axis_proposal.definition: Normalized change in high gamma power between 100-200 ms post-stimulus onset

statistics.note: Roughness calculated using MacCallum and Einbond (2008) algorithm based on Parncutt's dissonance model. Roughness values: consonant < dissonant I < dissonant II (p < 0.001, p < 0.01 respectively)

brain_regions.context: Positive correlation between γhigh power changes and roughness measures in both hemispheres

methodology.task: Passive listening to consonant and dissonant chords
methodology.control_conditions: Correlation analysis across all chord types

claims.claim_text: Consonant chords are rated as pleasant (mean rating 1.40 ± 0.16) and dissonant chords as unpleasant (mean rating -0.92 ± 0.33) in behavioral ratings, with significant difference in perceived valence

axis_proposal.definition: Subjective pleasantness rating on scale from -3 (very unpleasant) to +3 (very pleasant)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Behavioral measure of aesthetic/emotional response to consonance and dissonance

statistics.note: No significant difference in perceived valence between dissonant I (-0.85 ± 0.32) and dissonant II (-0.98 ± 0.36) chords, despite difference in roughness and neural activity

methodology.task: Pleasantness rating task (-3 to +3 scale) after hearing each chord
methodology.sample_size: 10 healthy subjects (6 males, 4 females; mean age 27.4 years, SD 2.72)

c3_associations.process: Differential processing of consonance and dissonance in superior temporal gyrus, with increased high gamma activity to dissonant chords reflecting sensory processing of roughness/beating
c3_associations.evidence: Increased γhigh activity (70-150 Hz) in STG in response to dissonant chords between 75-200 ms post-stimulus onset. Activity reflects sensory processing of roughness/beating generated by minor second and major seventh intervals. Positive correlation between γhigh power and stimulus roughness.

c3_associations.process: Consonance and dissonance elicit differential pleasantness ratings, with consonant chords rated as pleasant and dissonant chords as unpleasant
c3_associations.evidence: Behavioral ratings show consonant chords rated as pleasant (1.40 ± 0.16) and dissonant chords as unpleasant (-0.92 ± 0.33), with significant difference (p < 0.001). However, γhigh activity in STG is not strongly modulated by perceived valence, but rather by acoustical properties (roughness).

c3_associations.process: Spatial organization in right STG for pitch relationship processing, with anterior regions exhibiting increased sensitivity toward dissonant intervals
c3_associations.evidence: Spatial organization in right STG with dissonant-sensitive sites located anterior to non-sensitive sites. Consistent with hierarchical organization for pitch processing, where anterolateral regions are attuned to more complex pitch stimuli and pitch relationships.

primary_axes.note: Roughness values: consonant < dissonant I < dissonant II (0-7 scale)

r3_coordinates.note: High gamma power (DV) positively correlated with roughness (r = 0.43 RH, r = 0.41 LH). Roughness calculated using Parncutt's dissonance model.

mentions.context: 29 out of 32 electrodes (91%) with significant γhigh activity located within STG. Activity observed 50-350 ms post-stimulus onset
Spatial organization in right STG with dissonant-sensitive sites anterior to non-sensitive sites
Subset of dissonant-sensitive sites show increased activity to dissonant II chords
Positive correlation between γhigh power and roughness in both hemispheres
Spatial organization for pitch relationship processing
Dissonant-sensitive cortical sites located anterior to non-sensitive sites
Non-sensitive sites located posterior to dissonant-sensitive sites

brain_regions_summary.note: ECoG study with direct cortical recordings. All brain region mentions are from direct measurement. Spatial organization observed in right hemisphere but not left (possibly due to limited sample size: 3 left hemisphere, 5 right hemisphere subjects).

warnings: DV r3_axis unmapped in C01/C02/C05 (axis_proposal provided)
ECoG study with high temporal/spatial resolution but limited sample size (8 subjects, 3-5 electrodes per subject)
Spatial organization observed only in right hemisphere (left hemisphere may be due to limited sample size)

data_quality.note: High-quality ECoG study with direct cortical recordings. All brain regions collected with full context from direct measurement. R³ coordinates mapped for consonance/dissonance and roughness. Statistics well-reported with p-values and correlation coefficients.
